original_abstract: The chemical activation and hydrogenation of CO2 into methanol represent a promising strategy for the utilization of captured carbon. In this study, we investigate the performance of size-selected $Cu_4$ clusters supported on alumina thin films for $CO_2$ reduction. Catalytic activity was evaluated under near-atmospheric pressures with low $CO_2$ concentrations, while the oxidation states of the clusters were monitored via in situ grazing incidence X-ray absorption spectroscopy. Our findings demonstrate that $Cu_4$ clusters are exceptionally active, representing the most efficient catalyst reported to date for $CO_2$ conversion to $CH_3OH$ at low pressures. Theoretical analysis using density functional theory suggests that these clusters significantly lower the activation barrier for methanol synthesis. These results highlight the potential of sub-nanometer copper clusters as highly efficient catalysts for the sustainable recycling of $CO_2$.
humanized_abstract: Catalytic conversion of carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) into fuels and chemicals such as methanol (CH 3 OH) is considered as a viable method for utilizing the captured CO 2 . In this study, we have investigated the activity of size selected Cu 4 clusters supported on alumina thin film for the reduction of CO 2 to CH 3 OH under near-atmospheric pressure and low CO 2 concentration. The oxidation state of the clusters was probed in situ using grazing incidence X-ray absorption spectroscopy (GI-XAS). Cu 4 clusters showed unprecedented activity for the conversion of CO 2 into CH 3 OH at low pressure. Density functional theory (DFT) studies indicated that Cu 4 clusters facilitated a lower activation energy barrier for the formation of CH 3 OH. Sub-nanometer copper clusters hence hold a great promise as an efficient catalyst for the catalytic conversion of CO 2 into value-added fuels and chemicals.
